summary: The author benchmarks Python against Clojure, Julia, and Java on a text-processing task involving file I/O, string operations, and groupby operations on word pairs from 'Tale of Two Cities'. Surprisingly, Python performs as well as or better than the other languages, matching Java's performance at around 190-200ms. The experiment reveals that for text analytics involving dictionary/HashMap operations, Python's data structures are highly competitive, challenging the assumption that switching to lower-level or compiled languages will yield significant performance gains. The author concludes that the intuition from numerical computing—where type information provides major speedups—doesn't transfer well to text processing tasks dominated by dictionary lookups.
key_points: Python unexpectedly matches or beats Clojure, Julia, and even Java in text-processing benchmarks, running at 190-200ms versus Clojure's 550ms
The performance bottleneck in text analytics is often dictionary/HashMap implementations, where Python is well-optimized
Numerical computing intuition about types improving performance doesn't transfer well to text processing—explicit types in Java didn't provide meaningful speedups
Julia's installation has become surprisingly simple, removing a previous barrier to adoption
High-productivity languages (Python, Clojure, Julia) share the trait of not requiring explicit type information
money_quotes: The SciPy community has optimized the heck out of numerics; lets think about text!
Often we choose number 1, 'use more machines'. We make this choice because it's easy.
Can my 30-core shared memory machine or large distributed system be replaced with a few cores running tight compiled code?
I expected Python to be dead last. Instead it comfortably hums along in first.
As much as I love Clojure I have to say, that's an ugly lambda. It looks like a child mashing the top row of the keyboard.
Surprisingly, Java is not significantly faster. This provides us with a pretty stable lower bound for this problem.
Numerical computation intuition tells us that types are important. This intuition didn't carry over well to text and grouping.
I used to feel guilty about using Python for data processing. But this test gives me confidence in the performance of Python data structures.
key_insight: Python's performance disadvantage in numerical computing doesn't extend to text processing, where dictionary operations dominate and Python's data structures are highly competitive with compiled languages.
